impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products allegedly lack substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating Ingevity's defense under the patent laws.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors of patent holders benefit from reinforced protections against anticompetitive leveraging of patent rights into unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when structuring license agreements that require licensees to purchase unpatented goods, even where the patented and unpatented products are related. Companies must carefully assess whether tied products have substantial non-infringing uses.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was proper on claims of inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and Walker Process antitrust fraud for attempted monopolization when the patentee acquired prior art documents before filing its patent applications.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact. Tenaris had acquired documents describing a predecessor's coiled tubing product (CYMAX) before obtaining its own patents on similar technology. The court held that fact questions remained as to whether Tenaris's failure to disclose the CYMAX documents to the PTO constituted inequitable conduct and whether it rose to the level of fraud supporting an antitrust claim.
impacts.positiveImplications: Accused infringers retain robust ability to challenge patents obtained through alleged concealment of material prior art, including pursuit of antitrust damages under Walker Process.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire companies or assets containing prior art face heightened risk that failure to disclose those materials during prosecution could support both unenforceability and antitrust liability.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls to a model are directed to abstract ideas under Alice step one, and whether damages for foreign sales of cybersecurity software were properly awarded.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated the $185 million judgment, holding that Columbia's cybersecurity patents claiming emulator-based anomalous program detection are abstract at Alice step one, requiring remand for step two analysis. The court also held the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales. The ruling significantly impacts software patent enforcement and extraterritorial damages theories.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against software patent assertions benefit from the court's willingness to find cybersecurity detection methods abstract and to limit extraterritorial damages reach.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting software-implemented inventions, particularly in cybersecurity, face increased vulnerability to Alice eligibility challenges. Universities and research institutions monetizing software patents may see reduced damages when accused products are sold globally.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming server-side tailoring of content delivery specifications to match a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's streaming content patents were ineligible under § 101, reversing the jury's infringement verdict against Netflix and entering judgment for Netflix. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content presentation to device capabilities. This decision eliminates a $2.5 million damages award and underscores the continuing broad reach of Alice in the streaming technology space.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming platforms and content delivery networks gain stronger defenses against patent assertions covering adaptive content delivery, which the court views as abstract.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the content delivery and streaming technology space face significant obstacles to enforcing patents directed to device-adaptive content presentation.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent that missed the statutory deadline for a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) may obtain such a stay by voluntarily dismissing and refiling its declaratory judgment action.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not circumvent the statutory deadline for obtaining a mandatory stay of a parallel district court action by dismissing and refiling its declaratory judgment complaint. The court found this tactic inconsistent with § 1659(a)'s purpose of providing a limited window for mandatory stays during concurrent ITC and district court proceedings, preventing gamesmanship in parallel patent litigation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from certainty that ITC respondents cannot manipulate filing deadlines to obtain mandatory stays of district court proceedings through voluntary dismissal and refiling.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies facing concurrent ITC and district court proceedings must strictly comply with the 30-day statutory deadline for mandatory stays or lose the right to obtain one, with no ability to reset the clock through refiling.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple Watch models incorporating blood oxygen measurement features infringed Masimo's patents on wearable noninvasive physiological measurement devices.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's limited exclusion order barring importation of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen functionality, finding no error in the Commission's domestic industry determination, patent validity rulings, or infringement findings related to Masimo's pulse oximetry patents. This decision sustains a significant import ban on one of the world's most popular consumer electronics products.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the medical device and wearable technology space are reinforced in their ability to use ITC proceedings to obtain exclusion orders against major technology companies importing infringing products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies integrating health-monitoring features into consumer devices face substantial risk of import exclusion when those features are covered by existing medical device patents. Apple must continue modifying its products or face ongoing import restrictions.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in excluding a patent damages expert's report and granting summary judgment for absence of a remedy based on that exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent infringement case against Microsoft's Azure platform, vacating summary judgment entered on the ground that Exafer lacked a quantifiable remedy. The court found the district court applied an improperly heightened standard in evaluating the expert's methodology, reinforcing that Daubert gatekeeping should not function as a merits determination on damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from clarification that damages expert reports should not be excluded under Daubert based on overly stringent methodology requirements, particularly where the methodology has a reasonable basis.
impacts.negativeImplications: Large technology companies defending patent suits face reduced ability to obtain early summary judgment by challenging damages expert methodology, potentially extending costly litigation.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced patent infringement and erred in granting JMOL for the defendant in a medical device patent case.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and JMOL in favor of DePuy in a case alleging induced infringement of spinal surgery device patents. The court found the exclusions were an abuse of discretion and that sufficient evidence existed for a jury to find induced infringement. The case is remanded for a new trial where both experts may testify.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the medical device space benefit from a more permissive standard for expert testimony on induced infringement, making it harder for defendants to obtain pre-trial exclusion of infringement opinions.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device manufacturers face increased litigation exposure as the Federal Circuit signals courts should be less willing to exclude expert testimony on infringement, making early disposition more difficult.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review petitions constitute legislative rules requiring notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing IPR institution discretion are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under § 553(b). The court reasoned that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the Director retains ultimate authority over institution decisions, and the instructions do not purport to bind the Director. This preserves the PTO's broad discretion in declining to institute IPR proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's preserved ability to deny IPR petitions under flexible policy guidance, reducing exposure to post-grant patent challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners lose their challenge to PTO discretionary denial practices, meaning the Director can continue declining institution of meritorious petitions without formal rulemaking constraints.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed claims of a streaming media encryption patent in rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge in inter partes review.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction of DivX's partially-encrypted streaming media patent and vacated the Board's decision upholding patentability. The court adopted a different construction of key claim terms related to encryption information and remanded for the Board to reassess obviousness under the corrected construction. This decision may affect the validity of patents covering encryption in adaptive bitrate streaming systems.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming service providers benefit from a more favorable claim construction that could lead to invalidation of patents covering partial encryption of streaming content.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital content protection space face renewed invalidity challenges when appellate courts correct Board claim constructions in their favor.

impacts.caseName: National Veterans Legal Services Program v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that PACER fees exceeded statutory authority by funding non-PACER expenses. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to class representative nonprofits, rejecting a pro se objector's challenges. Hundreds of thousands of PACER users will receive full or substantial reimbursement for fees paid during the eight-year class period.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses, law firms, and other frequent PACER users benefit from substantial fee reimbursements and the establishment that PACER fees must correspond to actual system costs.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether restaurants that applied for but did not receive Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants have Tucker Act jurisdiction to sue the government for damages when the SBA failed to process applications in the statutorily required order.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants have Tucker Act jurisdiction to pursue damages claims against the government for unpaid RRF grants. The court held that the statutory language mandating the SBA 'shall award grants' in the order applications were received creates a money-mandating obligation enforceable in the Court of Federal Claims. This opens the door for potentially billions in claims by restaurants that were skipped in the grant process.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses denied government grants due to processing errors have a viable path to recover damages, reinforcing accountability for statutory grant distribution requirements.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potentially massive liability for failure to follow statutory mandates in distributing emergency relief funds, which could constrain future program design.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a means-plus-function claim limitation reciting 'control means' in a crop harvester patent is indefinite when the specification describes corresponding structure using terms like 'conventional in design and operation.'
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that the asserted claims of a crop harvester height-control patent were invalid as indefinite. The court held that the specification adequately disclosed corresponding structure for the 'control means' limitation, even though it described certain components as 'conventional,' finding that a person of ordinary skill would understand the disclosed structure. This reinstates the patent holder's infringement claims against Deere.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a more forgiving standard for means-plus-function claim definiteness, particularly where specifications describe structures in terms familiar to those skilled in the art.
impacts.negativeImplications: Agricultural equipment manufacturers and other accused infringers face reduced ability to invalidate means-plus-function claims based on specifications that use general terms to describe corresponding structures.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations as confidential is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in trade remedy investigations is incompatible with 19 U.S.C. § 1516a and does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial records. The court affirmed the CIT's order directing the ITC to comply with statutory confidentiality requirements on remand, requiring case-by-case assessment of whether information warrants confidential treatment.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and domestic producers benefit from increased transparency in trade remedy proceedings, gaining greater access to information underlying duty determinations that directly affect their businesses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations face increased risk that their commercially sensitive business information may be disclosed, as automatic confidentiality designations are no longer permissible.
